The Mungret Annual for 1906 is very well turned
out. The large number of illustrations and reproduc-
tions of photographs considerably enhance its interest
to all whom Mungret concerns. In view of his recent
experience in jail for five days because he would mnat
take out a license for his dog in Deupla, the excellent
portrait of €amon O Neitt has an interest all its
own. We observe that in the letter-press concerning
Mr. O’Neill he is styled Eddie O’Neill. We presume
that in the days between 1895 and 1901 whilst he was
at school he was known by that name. A great many
names have been changed, and a great many centres
of gravity have been shifted since then. We read in
th Annual:—“ Eddie O'Neill (1895-1901) is becoming
gradually more and more prominent as an enthusiastic
supporter of the principles of the Gaelic League. Early
in Novembr last he suffered a five days’ imprisonment
in Cork in order to record a protest against some of the
petty opposition which is being offered by Government

- representatives to the progress of the League. Eddie
refused to pay the fine of 6d. which was imposed at the
Kinsale Petty Sessions, for not having his dog registered
in the legal way—viz., with his name in English, and
elected to undergo the alternative penalty. On coming
up to Cork he was received at the railway station by an
enthusiastic crowd of citizens, who escorted him to his
honoured prison. The event ‘has attracted very much
- notice.”” 'We hope that all the old Mungret boys will
read that, and meditate on it, for we fear that some of
the old boys are West Britons. The Annual contains
a beautiful religious poem in Irish, and also a sketch in
Litst, * Mage Ni Cavla.”

“ Self-Government, and Popular Control,”’ according
to 'C.-B., is the foundation of the Liberal Government’s
policy towards Ireland— that those domestic affairs
which concern the Irish people only, and not ourselves,
should, as and when opportunity offers, be placed in
their hands (cheers). Down to last spring we had rea-
son to bolieve that cven the late Government and their
party had come round to the wisdom of such a policy.
They had already endowed the people of Ireland with
the command of County Government, they had pledged
112 millions of British credit to the tenants of Ireland,
and lastly, their Viceroy had been authorised to declare
that Ireland was henceforth to be governed according to
Irish ideas (hear, hear). They have started back from
that position ; but, oh! ladies and gentlemen, give them.
time; they cannot escape from the logic of their own
acts—(hear, hear)—and they will return to that which
is the path of justice and wisdom, and also of safety.’”
So said C.-B. But in pointing ~ut that Lord Dudley
had talked about governing Ireland according to Irish
ideas, he induces us to comment on the fact that he is
only talking about “ Self-Government and Popular Con-
trol.””  We are tired of talk. Let’s have a little self-
government at once. With all this fine talk going on,
are men still to be prosecuted for putting their names in
Irish only on their carts? are local bodies to be
jumped on by the over-paid Local Government Board
auditors for putting up the names of the streets in
Irish? Is the regulation concerning the proposed with-
drawal of the “ National’’ Board to be swept away?
Is Mr. Starkie to be kept as Resident Commissioner?
C.-B. and his Government had better lose no time in
doing something that may induce a much-deceived
people to place some value on a British minister’s talk.

Messrs. Malone Bros., Ltd., Great Strand Street, the
well-known blacking and polish manufacturers, have
sent us a sample of a new adhesive which they are manu-

scribed as a “ powerful -adhesive,”’ and the report we
have received on the specimen which we got tested
tallys with this description. A broken cigarette case
was, we understand, made serviceable again by the use
of the sample referred to.

One of the distinctive features of the Christmas sea-
son would be blotted out if some of our papers dismissed
their  Christmas-in-the-shops “ specials.”” Here they
are all at it again. We have just bought Pink on Fri-
day night. Let us see what Pink’s shop “ special ”’ has
been doing with himself to-day. What! The first
shop is a bungery ! —“ The handsomely fitted and excel-
lently appointed licensed establishment of is one
of those places where the public may enjoy with every
measure of comfort their moderate drink and their mid-
day luncheon. The liquors, stouts, ales, and all other
drinks are of the best, and are kept in the finest condi-
tion. The luncheon menu is vavied, and the cost is
very moderate. The establishment is under very care-
ful management, and the attendants are courteous and
obliging.”  How did Pink’s “ special ’ find out that
the drinks were “of the best?’

The next “ Christmas shop >’

moderate- cost, teeth ‘to fit well, to eat well, feel well,

the most exacting, and those who obtain such satisfac-
tion are cortain to feel thankful and grateful. Nothing
is more requisite to health and comfort, to say nothing
of “beauty, than good teeth.’’

who know both firms, would inform the public are both
contractors “ Idolators,”” or are both “ Saved,”” or is
the accepted “Saved” and the rejected “ Idolators.’”

Goulding would sanction such an abominable thing as
to allow religious considerations sway their actions in
such purely business matters.

Perhaps they are right after all in doggedly refusing
any inquiry into their mode of operations. Anxiously
awaiting some information regarding the religious tenets
of these two contractors,—I am, yours,

WAR.
I HAVE been much interested in the recent articles
in the LEADER on “ Anti-Enlistment.”” They

show that modern militarism is giving rise to question,
at least, in some minds. I was particularly struck by
one sentence in “Imaal’s’’ article. “ Formation
movements, education and industry—those are what we
want.””  And I heartily agree; for I am convinced that
the formation of an educated public opinion in favour
of an international tribunal for settling all disputes by
arbitration, and against all war, would lead to a higher
civilisation than any that has ever flourished. In such
a society the workers would be left free to enjoy the
fruit of their labours, instead of being taxed to starva-
tion point for the up-keep of vast military and naval
establishments.

Everyone reads the newspaper; and those who reflect;
at all on what they read cannot help being struck by
the fact that a very large part of the current news con-
sists of war news. Either two great and well-matched
nations are pitted against each other, asin the tremend
ous struggle just ended, or a powerful nation is using
all the resources of civilisation to overcome a small and
weak one, as in a conflict in recent years, or savage
tribes are trying to exterminate each other with the
help of the arms supplied by our commerce.

But it is not merely the papers which are taken up
with accounts of warfare; the story and the novel of the
day exactly resembles the paper in this respect.  And
after the paper such books are more read than anything
else, as the librarian of every public library can testify.
The ordinary novelist draws his material from military
doings—either in the barrack or the field. No hero is
so often put in a book as one wearing a uniform—in
fact, an epitome of a considerable number of tales
might be found in the verse of an old song:—

“They dressed me up in scarlet red,
And used me very kindly ;
But still I thought my heart would break
For the girl I left behind mol”

Stories for boys regale the readers with the hair-
breadth escapes of the hero from battle, murder, and
sudden death. Of course, I do not mean that because
popular fiction presents us with such pictures thaf,
therefore, we must accept them as a presentiment of
things as they really are. Not at all! Popular fiction
more generally presents life as it might, could, or would
he, under certain circumstances than as it is. Never-
theless, such fiction shows us the views and ideas which
are current at the time, and makes us acquainted with
the spivit of which war and fighting are regarded. And
the wholesale slaughter of onc’s fellows seems to be re-
garded as quite a thing, of course—as a necessity, and
not even “ a regrettable necessity.”” I remember when,
‘as a child, T read history, I accepted this state of affairs

THE LEADER. : 308

as normal. But it is some time since I put away childish
things, and began to ask—“ Why must people kill each !
other like beasts?’ But a satisfactory answer hae
never been forthcoming.

In the indictment against war, the waste involved

“ deserves very careful consideration. There is the waste

of lifo—our most precious possession. It is nob only
so in fact, but it is thus regarded in law. ‘When =
man wilfully deprives his neighbour of life, in steps the
law and takes the murderer’s life. I may say thaf I
think it rather illogical of society to imagine thei swe
blacks can make one white, or that the loss of one i
dividual’s life can be compensated for by taking ihe
life of another.

But the case at least serves to show that the taking of
human life is regarded as the most heinous crime, for
it is the only one in this country now punishable by the
extremo penalty. And yot at this moment, the various
states of Europe in time of peace, keep more than
4,000,000 men under arms. In time of war this mum-
ber can be augmented to 13,000,000. Our imagination
can hardly realise what such numbers mean. Here
are all those millions of men sot apart from the ordinary
avocations of life in order to learn to kill their fellow-
creatures. Physically, they are above the average of
the nation to which they belong, for strength amd
health are necessary to fitness for military service. In
other ways they are average people—no befter mor
worse when they enter the army, than others. They
have the same hopes and fears, and expectations as the
rest of us, their lives are as much to them, and they
are not maturally desirous of taking the lives of others.
But once in an army, the ordinary natural outlock upor
life, its duties and responsibilities, is changed. Instead
of earning a living by honest labour (which produces
for the community), the aim of the soldier must be %o
turn himself into an intelligent machine for dealing
death and distruction to human beings with the same
feelings and rights as himself. All the sentiments of =
common humanity—the pity, the tenderness, the sym-
pathy with pain, the regard for the condition of others
—must be more or less held in abeyance. And in war-
time, all the evil passions to which men are prone—
cruelty, sensuality, and greed—have scope and sway iz
a manner which does not bear thinking of.

Surely the masses of the people should refuse ic
allow themselves to be made any longer the pawns in
the great game of ambition, which is being played by
those in high places! The game has its prizes, ne
doubt, but they do not fall to the slaughtered thou-
sands, or to those who work and weep at home. f®
the words of an Irish poet, “ Now I pray that all ware
may cease!’’

THE HUMBUG OF IRISH DEYELOPMENY.

HI most terrible problem which faces Ireland on
T the material side is the increased and increasing
cost of her home government. We simply don’t realise
what is happening, and no one troubles fo peint it out
to us. We hear, indeed, of increased taxation due to
the Boer War, but the astonishing truth is that Treland
is not paying a single penny extra owing to that cam-
paign.  Our Imperial tribute, out of which all military
charges come, averaged two and a-half millions during
the last five years, which is slightly wnder the amount
(£2,676,000) of the same tribute ten years before in
the year 1889-90. But, on the other hand, the t of
the government in Dublin increased by two millions in
the ten years between 1890 and 1900, and has increased
hy a further half million in the last five years. In 1890
the cost of Irish government was 5 millions, to-day it is
74 millions ; in fifteen years the cost of Castle rule has
tnereased by one-half!  In fact, though the Beer Wa
cost us nothing, our taxes have increased in the same
proportion as English taxes were increased by that cam-
paign, and, owing to home extravagance, we dre just
in the same position as if, sometime in the nineti S, we
had heen conducting a war (costing, say, £100,000,000)
on our own account !
There is not the smallest exaggeration in
atement, and I propose to show how es
is done. The figures I take from an o little
book on ‘‘ Ireland’s Burden,” by Mr. C. Lehane, but
I use only rough approximations, so as to make the
statment clear. Here is what the departments cost,
and how the money is spent. Or, to put it in another
way, if we had been conquered a second time zbout the
date of the second Home Rule Bill and had to pay two
Imperial tributes, one to England and another of equal
amount, say, to France, we would be just in the posi-
tion we actually are at present.

ARMAGH.

‘T HE paragraph in a recent week’s Leaber referring

{ to the Armagh bookseller’s list revealed a state
-o_f affairs which is typical of things as they ave in this
citadel of Gael and Gall which we in Armagh are wont
to boast of as the ecclesiastical capital of Ireland.

Mr. Greer is neither a Nationalist nor an Irish Ire-
lander, and, to do him justice, he does not pose as one
—quite the reverse—thereby comparing favourably
with a number of leading lights here of whom better
might be expected; but, being a man of some business
instinet, the heavy Scotch variety found so plentifully
in the North, and lately introduced in experimental
quantities all over the other provinces, he seized the op-
portunity, afforded by the recent changes brought about
in the National Board’s book department, to establish an
agency for the supply of books to National Schools. He
found latterly that there was a good demand for Irish
books, and took steps to meet it. Hence the famous
catalogue.

The reader who does not understand Armagh will
wonder why, then, did he not learn the Irish alphabet
so as to have the book-titles correctly set out on his list.
It is that he belongs to a class which heartily despises
the movement and all its supporters, yet is willing to
take advantage of its results.

In Armagh those who are not in sympathy with the
Irish Ireland Movement are the quickest to take advan-
tage of the slightest opportunity to profit by it, while
those who might naturally be supposed to feel its
slightest pulsation stand idly by complaining of the lack
of support from Irish Irelanders, and never think of
exerting themselves to deserve, encourage, or foster that
support when it is given to them.

The nominal Irish Irelanders in Armagh who are en-
gaged in shop-keeping do not understand the possibili-
ties of the movement, and are utterly incapable of being
taught until it is too late. 3

The consequence is, that their businessis, as a rule, of
the twopence-halfpenny degree, which is unable to meet
an order except in a huxtering way, accompanied very
often by inexcusable delay and slovenliness; and, in-
deed, it is very doubtful whether any effort for their in-
dustrial redemption would not be a sheer waste of
energy. .

An Trish class has been in operation off and on for
some years, but its influence for good in the direction
indicated has practically been nil, because the people
of Armagh—as a body—hold aloof from it. Why this
is so is due to a variety of causes brought about by
decades of Anglicisation ; yet the chicf one is the despic-
able idea pervading all Armagh socicty from Bum Bee
Entry and Poor School Lane up to tho gentle slopes of

Banbrook Hill, that “ You're not as good as me, and

"T’H not rub clothes with you.”” The key of the situa-
tion is that “ X,”” the owner of the public-house at the
corner, whose great-grandfather may, perhaps, have
gathered rags or hawked tins, will not work for TIreland
alongside “'Y,” whosk equally remote ancestor may
have sung hallads at a fair.

It is true that the Irish classes are well attended ab
prosent, but the membership consists principally of
juveniles—and some National teachers of whom much

- ~cannot be expected for a long time to come, as they are

THE ERASMUS SYITH ENDOWMENT—XVI.
ProresTant Law anxp Logcic.

T N the last article I have proved two canons or prin-
g ciples of evidence, namely :—(1) We are to admit
no intention in Erasmus Smith of which we have not
cvidence. (2) Any thing that Erasmus Smith was com-
pelled to do by © the laws of the day ’—of his day—is
not evidence of his intention.

The first of these canons is also laid down by Tudor,
p. 117, where he says:—“ What the intention of the
founder or founders was is a question of fact.”” And
being a question of fact, must, of course, be proved
by evidence.

I have shown that the Law Lords, in the case of the
Free Church of Scotland, have laid down the first canon
of evidence in the precise form and sense in which T
use it in this argument, namely, we are to admit no
religious intention in Erasmus Smith, and no conse-
quent religious trust imposed by him on his Endow-
ment, as a ground for transferring the property. of his
Endowment from the beneficiaries specifically designated
by him, except they, both the intention and the trust,
are proved by evidence.

By the second canon of evidence laid down by Lord
Justice Fitzgibbon, namely, any thing that Erasmus
Smith was compelled to do by the laws of his day is
not evidence of his intention, I shall prove that there
is not a particle of evidence to prove that Erasmus
Smith had any religious intention at all in making his
foundation, or that he imposed on his endowment any
religious trust. I shall prove that there is not a par-
ticle of evidence to show that he had any intention of
his own to impress on his Endowment either the Puri-
tan character under Cromwell, or the Episcopalian
character under Charles IT., as he was ¢ compelled by
the laws of the day’’ to impress the former character
on his Endowment under Cromwell, and the latter
character under Charles IT.

Protestants attempt to prove that Erasmus Smith
intended to impress the Puritan, or auti-Episcopalihn
character on his endowment by the four following
facts : —

1. He appointed only Puritan Trustees by the Inden-
ture under Cromwell.

2. He directed to appoint only Puritan Schoolmasters
by the Indenture.

3. Ho directied by the Tudenture only Puritan prayers
and Puritan instructions out of the Puritan Catechism
that of |:ht_> ‘Westminster divines. 4

4. He dirccted by the Indenture that the pupils of his
schools should go to Trinity College, which was Puritan
at the time, and admitted only Puritans.

i

. Now, in order to prove that these four acts supply no
‘evidence of a Puritan Intention in Erasmus Smith, I

~ have only to show that he was compelled by “the lz\iws
of the day ’’ to do each and all of these four acts. Be-
‘cause by the second canon of evidence laid down by
“Lord Justice Fitzgibbon, anything he was compelled to
«do by “the laws of the day ™ is not evidence of his in-
‘tention.

Regarding the first act, there is not a word in the In-
‘denture directing that the Trustees should bo Puritans,
but we know from the history and law of the time, that
-only Puritans could be Trustees.

Regarding the second act, there is no express direc-
‘tion in the Indenture that the schoolmasters should bo
Puritan, but from the duties therein assigned to them,
we infer that they should bo. For they should pray
with their pupils-——and extempore prayor was part of

THE PARISH PRIEST OF KURTSCHEID.*

IVE years have now passed since the LEADER
began to breathe life into the Irish pgople. Eor‘
long they had sunk into the torpor of stupid despair,
and when they were told of the weakness of the verb
“to say,”” and the strength of- the very “to do,” they
wero startled. One would think that the new learning
must have blossomed into a wholesome fruit; and it is
disappointing that more has not been done.

The want of example, perhaps, and the abundance of
precept may account for the lack of fruit; and those
whose philosophy makes them look eastwards, or west-
wards, or anywhere but in themselves, before beginning
to do the least thing, may find in the little booklet pub-
lished recently by the Catholic Truth Society that in-
centive which they require to risk and do something,
for that country of which they simulate so much love, to
make it a little brighter, a little happier, and a littie
more prosperous.

The scene of the labours of Father Schiitz is laid in
that romantic region bordering both banks of the
Rhine and Lower Moselle. To Kurtscheid, an unimpor-
tant village in this district, Father Schiitz was sent as
Parish Priest in the year 1877. The picture of the
place as it stood then is shocking almost, to one who
cherishes golden memecries of old Rhine fairy tales.

“When I for the first time set foot in the friendly
village, and paid my first visit to the Church, I ob
served in the latter a melancholy poverty and emp
ness. Hardly an attempt at ornament; hardly an ob-
ject of devotion; half-blind windows, decayed benches
of the most uncomfortable pattern, a sort of hen-roost
for a gallery, and a pavement of unhewn flags; with.
to crown the whole, a wooden steeple and worm-eaten
wooden roof like the roof of a barn. At a short dis-
tance to the right, on a stony hillock bare of vegeta-
tion, stood a plain-looking cottage. This was the paro-
ciiial residence.’’

It may well be supposed that the heart of an Angli-
cised Irishman would be daunted by such a sight; and
when he had learned that the poor villagers had to con-
tribute not only towards the support of a neighbouring
pastor, but had also to pay tithes to the evangelical
clergyman, in all probability he would think that no-
thing short of an angel sliding down a sunbeam could
bring prosperity to Kurtscheid. The German priest.
however, whose mind had been nurtured on its native
speech, looked the blackest things in the face till he had
sized them up, and then resolved to brighten them.
‘With the object of paying off parochial debts Father
Schiitz opened a school with a nucleus of four pupils.
It grew until it became famous, and as suddenly it was
shattered by jealousy and bigotry.

Its non-success was a triumph of failure. When the
care of the school was lifted off his shoulders, his
energy ran in the direction of agriculture. He had
long been of opinion that much of the depressed state
of the rural districts was due to the effete traditional
methods of farming employed by the landowners. To
these methods they clung tenaciously. The lectures of
an agricultural expert were not enough to make them
loosen their hold. The Parish Priest had to try an-
other expedient. He started farming himself, gathered
information from the people themselves, and out of
this he built a scientific system. This system he ex-
plained in a course of “ agricultural sermons > which he
delivered. The eyes of the people were opened— bare
stretches of heather were now rapidly transformed into
fruitful meadows. The soil is, in many ‘places on these
mountain farms, no more than a foot in depth over the
barren rock.  Nevertheless, every available patclr of
carth was placed under cultivation, and turned into
productive tillage land.”

Everyone, however, could not be a farmer, and work
must be found for the idle hands of the peasant boys
and girls. If possible this work must be actually im or,
ab least, near their homes Some energetic canvassing
among neighbouring capitalists found employment for
